title: Mandatory security training — completion SLA by role
exclusions: Does NOT evaluate training quality or effectiveness; see UC-22.46.2.
evidence: Weekly 'training_completion_sla' writes completion evidence per role to compliance_summary.

compliance.assurance_rationale: §164.308(a)(5) security awareness and training — completion evidence is required.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that HIPAA Security §164.308(a)(5) (Security awareness and training) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.46.1: Mandatory security training — completion SLA by role.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.46.1' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: PCI 12.6 security awareness — the UC evidences completion at cadence.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that PCI DSS 12.6 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.46.1: Mandatory security training — completion SLA by role.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(2)(g) cyber hygiene and training — completion tracking is standard evidence.

controlTest.positiveScenario: When an employee in an in-scope role has no completion record for the annual mandatory course within the 365-day window, the UC fires with employee, role, last_completion.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Employees with a completion in the current cycle OR employees on approved leave do NOT fire.

dataSources: See subcategory preamble. — supplemented by authentication logs, system audit trails, and configuration management database (CMDB) asset inventory exports
spl:
index=lms sourcetype=completion earliest=-365d course_id=security_awareness
| stats max(_time) as last_completion by employee_id
| lookup hr_roster.csv employee_id OUTPUT role status
| where status="active" AND (isnull(last_completion) OR (now()-last_completion)>31536000)
| table employee_id role last_completion
description: Produces per-employee completion evidence for the mandatory annual training. Gaps are surfaced in time for HR to re-enroll before the cycle closes.
value: Moves completion from a retrospective export to a continuously-known KPI.
implementation: (1) Ingest LMS events; (2) refresh hr_roster.csv nightly; (3) run weekly; (4) on finding, auto-enrol via HR integration. Indexes required: `index=lms`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=completion`.
visualization: Bar chart of completion by department, table of overdue, single-value '% completion in cycle'. Key fields: `employee_id`, `last_completion`, `role`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Break-the-glass emergency access** — Clinicians exercising documented emergency access procedures to ePHI during patient safety events generate elevated access alerts that are legitimate under the HIPAA treatment, payment, and operations (TPO) exception.

2. **Health information exchange (HIE) activity** — Automated ePHI transmissions through approved health information exchanges and interoperability interfaces produce cross-boundary data flows that are authorised under business associate agreements.

3. **Clinical research data extraction** — IRB-approved research protocols involve de-identified or limited data set extractions that trigger data access monitoring rules but are covered by the approved research data use agreement.

4. **System backup and archival processes** — Scheduled ePHI backup operations, media rotation, and long-term archival procedures generate bulk data access events that are operationally required and documented in the information lifecycle policy.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `hipaa_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved break-glass event IDs, HIE partner identifiers, IRB protocol numbers, and scheduled backup job identifiers. The correlation search filters against this lookup and suppresses matches where `hipaa_exception_approved=true`.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `lms` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `hr_roster.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('hr_roster.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `§164.308(a)(5)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that HIPAA Security §164.308(a)(5) (Security awareness and training) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.46.1: Mandatory security training — completion SLA by role." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `lms` under `completion`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Ingest LMS events. Ingestion SLA drift is usually invisible until it breaks something — monitor the feed's freshness with a companion search that alerts when the newest event is older than the expected cadence.

• **Hint 2:** refresh hr_roster.csv nightly. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** run weekly. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** on finding, auto-enrol via HR integration. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Mandatory security training — completion SLA by role," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the HIPAA Security control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search enriches raw events with context from the lookup `hr_roster.csv` — the enrichment table that adds compliance-relevant business context to raw events. The raw event stream alone cannot answer the compliance question because it does not carry the authoritative business attributes (`role status`) — these live in a register maintained outside the monitored system. The `| lookup` joins at search time on `employee_id`, making the search results only as accurate as the lookup's freshness.

Lookup ownership and freshness: This is the single most common failure mode for lookup-based compliance searches. The `hr_roster.csv` table must be owned by a named team with a documented refresh cadence and staleness alerting. A minimum operational SLA is: (1) lookup refreshed at a cadence matching the risk domain (daily for access control, weekly for inventory, quarterly for vendor registers), (2) automated alert when the lookup has not been rewritten in 2x the refresh interval, (3) a row-count sanity check (alert if row count changes by more than 20% between refreshes — catches truncation bugs in upstream exports).

Case sensitivity: Splunk's lookup matching is case-sensitive by default. If the raw event field and the lookup key differ in case (e.g. `Account_Name=JSMITH` vs lookup `samaccountname=jsmith`), the join silently returns null. Normalize both sides with `| eval employee_id = lower(employee_id)` before the lookup, or set `case_sensitive_match = false` in the lookup's `transforms.conf` stanza. Search across the last year.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.46.1: Mandatory security training — completion SLA by role]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -365d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.46.1,reg=HIPAA_Security
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=lms sourcetype=completion earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `employee_id`, `last_completion`, `role`. Run:

```spl
index=lms sourcetype=completion earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table employee_id, last_completion, role
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `hr_roster.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup hr_roster.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.46.1: HIPAA Security evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Bar Chart Of Completion By Department (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Table Of Overdue (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single-Value '% Completion In Cycle' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** Check the lookup's case sensitivity (Splunk lookups are case-sensitive by default) and null handling — a null on the input side produces a silent miss, not an error.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**HIPAA Security-specific failure modes:**

• Minimum-necessary principle: access-anomaly detections (excessive chart views, unexpected departments) must NOT flag clinicians accessing records for treatment purposes — §164.502(b) explicitly permits this. Join detections against the clinician's current department and patient's active encounter to avoid generating thousands of false positives on ED doctors seeing admitted patients.

• Break-glass and emergency access (§164.312(a)(2)(ii)): emergency accounts will legitimately bypass normal access controls during downtime events. Maintain a lookup `breakglass_declarations.csv` written by the clinical informatics on-call; correlate every break-glass access against a declared event — un-declared emergency access is the finding.

• Audit log retention (§164.312(b)): HIPAA requires 6 years of audit log retention, longer than most Splunk index default settings. Verify `frozenTimePeriodInSecs >= 189216000` for every index named in an HIPAA UC's search, and that the archive tier (S3, cold storage) is provisioned.

• Covered vs non-covered entity scope: if the search unintentionally pulls data from a non-covered business unit (e.g. a research arm not operating as a HIPAA Covered Entity), findings are noise. Filter using the facility/cost-center lookup that the Privacy Officer maintains, not by index name alone.
grandmaExplanation: We help you see who still needs training so awareness stays current across teams and roles.
